In this world, a dispute about creativity has already reached a delicate point. One side has argued that large language models fail because the relevant search spaces are intractable. But an anomaly immediately follows: humans also do not consciously inspect vast search spaces. So if brute intractability is offered as the explanation for failure in one case, it threatens to explain away human creativity too. The argument can no longer cleanly separate machine limitation from human achievement merely by invoking combinatorial explosion.

A second pressure enters. The architecture under discussion appears to rely on candidate generation and criticism proceeding mostly in language. Yet Einstein’s route from a “happiest thought” to field equations took ten years of mathematics. If that episode is representative, then verbal iteration is not obviously an adequate substrate for all major discovery. The implication is not that language is useless, but that a language-centered architecture may omit exactly the sustained formal transformation that some breakthroughs require. So the theory is squeezed from both sides: intractability is too indiscriminate, and language may be too weak.

A third tension sharpens the problem. There are “universal pools” and “novel pools.” A universal background prior is attractive because it supplies broad coverage. But the more universal it becomes, the more it risks flattening the rare, domain-specific weirdness from which actual discovery may arise. A system tuned toward generality may therefore erase the outliers it most needs to preserve. The architecture must then choose: either keep a broad prior and risk suppressing discovery-relevant oddity, or admit specialized, less universal reservoirs and give up some of its claim to unified generality.

These conceptual stresses are not unfolding in a calm forum. The controversial representatives retreated after someone tossed the cupboard. Whatever the cupboard is—a prop, fixture, or symbolic object—its being tossed changes the social geometry of the debate. The representatives withdraw rather than continue defending their position under those conditions. Their retreat suggests either vulnerability, loss of legitimacy, or a breakdown in the setting needed to sustain argument.

At the same time, onstage, the waterproof sect ceased confronting its enemies. That cessation mirrors the retreat. Confrontation stops. The stage remains, but antagonism is suspended. This does not resolve the intellectual tensions; it merely halts their public collision.

So the resulting world is one in which a theory of creativity faces internal logical strain, its defenders have retreated after a disruptive event, and even its ritualized opponents have stopped fighting. The deepest implication is not victory for any side. It is stalemate: the easy explanation has been destabilized, the proposed architecture looks incomplete, the tradeoff between universality and weirdness remains live, and the social machinery of confrontation has temporarily broken down.
